walkthrough.title: Choose Your Color
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Pictomania! First things first — everyone picks a color. There are six colors to choose from. When you grab a color, you take the pencil, all seven guessing cards, and the scoring tokens in that color. Return any unchosen colors to the box.

walkthrough.title: Grab Your Drawing Paper
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a piece of drawing paper from the pad. Fold it in half to create a little booklet with four pages — one page for each of the four rounds. You will write your score inside the star on each page after scoring.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Scoring Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place your colored scoring tokens on the table to the left side of your drawing paper. How many tokens you use depends on the number of players: in a 3-player game, use 2 tokens. In a 4-player game, use 3. In a 5-player game, use 4. And in a 6-player game, use 5. Return any unused tokens to the box.

walkthrough.title: Sort Your Guessing Cards
walkthrough.text: You get all 7 guessing cards regardless of player count. These are numbered 1 through 7, and you will use them to guess what other players are drawing. Pro tip: sort them by number right now. During the game, speed matters, and fumbling through unsorted cards will cost you precious seconds.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Word Cards
walkthrough.text: For the first round, you only need the green word cards — those are the easiest difficulty. Shuffle the green deck, draw 3 cards at random, and place them in the card holder. Set the rest of the green deck aside. The holder should go where everyone can comfortably see and read all three cards.

walkthrough.title: Read the 21 Words
walkthrough.text: Each word card has 7 words numbered 1 through 7. With three cards in the holder, that is 21 possible words for this round. Take a minute so everyone can read through all 21 words. Notice that words on the same card tend to be related — that is what makes guessing tricky!

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Black Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the 7 black number cards (numbered 1-7) and the 6 black letter cards (A, B, C). Keep these as two separate decks and shuffle each deck independently. These are used to secretly assign each player a word to draw.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Black Scoring Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the black scoring tokens in the center of the table. The number of black tokens depends on your player count: 3 players use 3 tokens, 4 players use 4, 5 players use 5, and 6 players use 6. Return any unused black tokens to the box. These are bonus points you grab when you finish guessing.

walkthrough.title: Orient Your Paper
walkthrough.text: One last thing before you start. You can orient your drawing paper either horizontally or vertically — whatever suits your drawing style. Just make sure the gray line is at the bottom so other players can tell which way is up. Some people draw wide, some draw tall. Pictomania does not judge.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play!
walkthrough.text: Your table should now have: the card holder with 3 green word cards in the middle, black scoring tokens in the center, and each player set up with their colored pencil, guessing cards, scoring tokens, and folded drawing paper. The two shuffled black decks are ready to deal. You are all set for round one!

summary.title: Ready to Play!
bullets: Card holder with 3 word cards in the middle
Black scoring tokens in center, two shuffled black decks ready
Each player: pencil, sorted guessing cards, scoring tokens, drawing paper

walkthrough.title: Four Rounds of Chaos
walkthrough.text: Pictomania is played over four rounds. Each round uses a different difficulty deck of word cards: green (easiest), yellow, orange, and red (hardest). You can mix and match difficulties to suit your group — four rounds of green is totally fine for kids or first-timers. Just keep a discard pile so cards do not repeat across rounds.

walkthrough.title: Getting Your Secret Word
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, deal one black letter card and one black number card to each player. These are SECRET — do not show anyone! Your letter card tells you which of the three word cards in the holder is yours (A, B, or C). Your number card tells you which of the seven words on that card to draw (1 through 7). Once you know your word, place both black cards face down in a stack in front of your drawing paper.

walkthrough.title: Simultaneous Drawing
walkthrough.text: Here is what makes Pictomania special — everyone draws at the SAME TIME. There are no turns. As soon as everyone has seen their word, start drawing! Your goal is to draw a picture that represents your word but NOT the other similar words on your card. Remember, the words on each card are deliberately related. If your word is 'guitar' but 'violin' and 'drum' are also on the card, draw something unmistakably guitar-shaped. Use the whole page — bigger drawings are easier to see from across the table.

walkthrough.title: Guessing While Drawing
walkthrough.text: Here is the wild part — you do not wait for everyone to finish drawing before you start guessing! As soon as you think you know what another player is drawing, you can make a guess. Put your pencil down, look at the word cards, find the word you think they are drawing, and place the matching numbered guessing card face down on the stack of cards beside their drawing. Speed matters — earlier guesses earn more points!

walkthrough.title: Guessing Rules
walkthrough.text: A few important guessing rules. You can only make ONE guess per player's drawing — no changing your mind once a guessing card is placed. You should NEVER guess your own number (since each player has a unique number). You do not have to guess everyone's drawing, but it usually pays to try. If you realize you need a guessing card number that you already played on someone else, you have made a mistake — tough luck, but that is part of the game!

walkthrough.title: Finishing and the Black Token
walkthrough.text: When you are done drawing and guessing, take one of the black scoring tokens from the center of the table. You will probably want the one with the most stars — but any token is legal. Once you grab a black token, you are done for the round. Sit back and watch the remaining players sweat! The round ends when the last player takes a black scoring token.

walkthrough.title: Revealing and Scoring Guesses
walkthrough.text: Time to see who nailed it! Each player flips over the stack of cards beside their drawing. Your letter and number cards are on top, revealing your secret word. Below them are the other players' guesses, in order from first to last. Go through each guess: if correct, give that player your scoring token with the most remaining stars. If wrong, place the guessing card in the center of the table. Keep going through the stack until all guesses are scored.

walkthrough.title: Calculating Your Score
walkthrough.text: Your score has two sides — literally. On the RIGHT side of your drawing, place tokens you earned from correct guesses on other drawings, plus your black scoring token. These are your POSITIVE points. On the LEFT side, any of your OWN colored tokens that were not given away (because players failed to guess your word) remain. These count as NEGATIVE points. Add up the stars on the right, subtract the stars on the left. Write your score in the star on your drawing paper. Yes, your score can go negative!

walkthrough.title: The Black Sheep Rule
walkthrough.text: Here is the punishment for wild guessing! The player with the MOST wrong guesses in a round is the 'black sheep.' If you are the black sheep, move your black scoring token to the LEFT side — it now counts AGAINST you instead of for you. However, if multiple players are tied for most wrong guesses, there is no black sheep and everyone keeps their black token on the right. Also, if nobody correctly guessed your word AND you are not the black sheep, return your black token to the center — it scores nothing.

walkthrough.title: Next Round and Game End
walkthrough.text: After scoring, everyone takes back their guessing cards and colored scoring tokens. Leave the black tokens in the center. For round two, shuffle the yellow deck and draw 3 new cards. Before dealing black cards, make sure everyone understands every word — replace any unknown word cards. After four rounds, fold your drawing paper to see all four scores and add them up. Highest total wins! There is no tiebreaker, so feel free to debate whose drawings were the coolest.

Keep a discard pile so word cards don't repeat

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Round!
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a complete round of Pictomania with four example players: Red, Blue, Green, and Yellow. We will use the green (easiest) word cards. Even if you have more or fewer players, follow along — this will teach you everything you need to know. Ready? Grab your pencils!

walkthrough.title: The Word Cards Are Up
walkthrough.text: Three green word cards are in the holder. Everyone takes a minute to scan all 21 words. Notice how the words on each card are related. Card A might have animals, Card B might have vehicles, Card C might have food items. The similarity within each card is what makes this game hilariously tricky — your drawing needs to nail YOUR word without looking like its neighbors.

walkthrough.title: Dealing the Secret Words
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the letter cards and number cards separately. Deal one of each to every player. Red gets letter B and number 3. Blue gets letter A and number 5. Green gets letter C and number 1. Yellow gets letter A and number 2. Everyone peeks at their cards and places them face down in a stack in front of their paper. Each player now knows which word to draw — and nobody else knows what it is!

walkthrough.title: Everyone Starts Drawing!
walkthrough.text: Three, two, one — GO! All four players start drawing simultaneously. Red is frantically sketching their word. Blue is drawing big, filling the whole page. Green is making tiny detailed marks in the corner. Yellow is drawing confidently with bold lines. The room fills with the sound of pencils scratching and nervous laughter. This is Pictomania!

walkthrough.title: Blue Finishes First and Starts Guessing
walkthrough.text: Blue puts down the pencil and looks around the table. Blue thinks Red's drawing looks like word number 3 on card B. Blue finds guessing card #3 and places it face down on Red's stack. Then Blue looks at Green's drawing and takes a guess there too. Blue is racking up guesses while the others are still drawing!

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Mistake: Guessing Too Hastily
walkthrough.text: Yellow finishes drawing and immediately starts guessing. Yellow glances at Red's half-finished drawing and thinks it looks like word #5. Yellow slaps down guessing card #5 on Red's stack. But wait — Yellow was assigned number 2, not number 5. That is fine, you CAN guess number 5. The problem is that Yellow barely looked at the drawing and is just guessing wildly. Hasty guesses lead to the black sheep penalty!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Look Before You Guess
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple: take a moment to actually study the drawing before committing your guess. Once a guessing card is placed, you cannot take it back. Wild guessing might occasionally get lucky, but more often it racks up wrong guesses and earns you the black sheep penalty. A wrong guess costs you nothing directly — but being the player with THE MOST wrong guesses means your black token flips negative.

walkthrough.title: Green Finishes and Makes Careful Guesses
walkthrough.text: Green puts down the pencil and studies each drawing carefully. Green looks at the word cards, cross-references with the drawings, and makes deliberate guesses. Green places a guessing card on Red's stack, then on Blue's, then on Yellow's. Green is the last to guess but feels confident about every choice.

walkthrough.title: Grabbing the Black Tokens
walkthrough.text: As each player finishes guessing, they grab a black scoring token from the center. Blue finished first and snagged the 5-star token — nice! Red grabbed the 3-star token. Yellow took the 2-star token. Green, being last, gets the 1-star token. The round is now over. Time to score!

walkthrough.title: Revealing Red's Word
walkthrough.text: Red flips the card stack. Letter B, Number 3 — now everyone knows Red's word. Blue's guess is on top (they guessed first). Blue guessed #3 — correct! Red hands Blue the highest remaining colored scoring token (the 5-star token). Next is Yellow's guess: Yellow guessed #5 — wrong! Yellow's guessing card goes to the center of the table. Green guessed #3 — correct! Red gives Green the next scoring token (3-star).

walkthrough.title: Revealing the Other Words
walkthrough.text: The process repeats for Blue, Green, and Yellow's drawings. Each player flips their stack, reveals their word, and goes through the guesses in order. Correct guesses earn scoring tokens (highest first). Wrong guesses go to the center. Pay attention to how many wrong guess cards pile up in the center — this determines the black sheep!

walkthrough.title: Yellow's Mistake Bites Back: Black Sheep
walkthrough.text: After scoring all four stacks, let's count wrong guesses. Blue has 0 wrong. Red has 1 wrong. Green has 0 wrong. Yellow has 2 wrong — the most! Yellow is the black sheep this round. Yellow's black scoring token (2 stars) now moves to the LEFT side of the drawing. Instead of earning 2 points, Yellow LOSES 2 points. Ouch!

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Black Sheep Penalty Stings
walkthrough.text: The black sheep rule is Pictomania's way of punishing wild guessing. It is a double hit — not only do your wrong guesses mean you missed out on correct-guess tokens, but your black token now counts AGAINST you. The penalty is especially brutal with high-star black tokens. Bottom line: it is better to skip a guess you are unsure about than to guess wrong and risk becoming the black sheep.

walkthrough.title: Calculating Everyone's Scores
walkthrough.text: Let's tally up. Blue earned tokens from correct guesses on the right (say 8 stars total including the black token), and gave away all colored tokens (0 on left). Blue scores 8! Red earned 5 stars on the right and has 1 unawarded token on the left (2 stars). Red scores 3. Green earned 6 stars on the right and 0 on the left. Green scores 6. Yellow earned 3 stars on the right, has 1 unawarded token (1 star) and the black sheep penalty (2 stars) on the left. Yellow scores 0. Everyone writes their score in the star on the first page.

walkthrough.title: Green's Mistake: Drawing Too Small
walkthrough.text: Wait — why did Green have unawarded tokens? Because Green drew tiny detailed marks in the corner of the page. The drawing was technically great, but nobody could see it from across the table! Green's artistic talent was wasted because the drawing was too small to read at a distance.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Draw BIG!
walkthrough.text: This is a party game played around a table. Other players are looking at your drawing from several feet away. Use the ENTIRE page. Bold lines, simple shapes, big features. This is not an art competition — clarity beats beauty every time. The fastest, sloppiest drawing that is obviously recognizable will outscore a beautiful tiny masterpiece that nobody can identify.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up Round Two
walkthrough.text: Take back all your guessing cards and colored scoring tokens. Leave the black tokens in the center. Now swap the green word cards for the yellow deck (medium difficulty). Shuffle, draw 3 new cards, and place them in the holder. Give everyone time to read the new 21 words. Shuffle and re-deal the black letter and number cards. Ready for round two!

walkthrough.title: Congratulations!
walkthrough.text: You have survived your first round of Pictomania! You have seen simultaneous drawing and guessing in action, watched Yellow learn about the black sheep penalty the hard way, and learned that drawing big and clear beats drawing pretty. Three more rounds to go — each one gets more fun as the words get trickier.

walkthrough.title: Before You Draw Again
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into your next round, here are three things to remember.

First — check out General Tips for drawing and guessing strategy.

Second — reference the Appendix for scoring details and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun drawing!

summary.title: Before You Draw Again
bullets: Check out General Tips for drawing and guessing strategy
Reference the Appendix for scoring details and game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Drawing: Differentiate, Don't Decorate
walkthrough.text: The number one mistake new players make is trying to draw a beautiful picture of their word. Wrong approach! Your job is not to draw your word — your job is to draw your word in a way that rules out the other words on the same card. If your card has 'bicycle,' 'motorcycle,' and 'car,' don't draw a generic vehicle. Draw pedals, a chain, and handlebars — the things that ONLY a bicycle has. Always ask yourself: what makes my word different from the others?

walkthrough.title: Speed vs. Accuracy in Guessing
walkthrough.text: Pictomania rewards being fast but punishes being reckless. The first correct guesser gets the highest-value token, so speed has real value. But wrong guesses accumulate toward the black sheep penalty. The sweet spot is to make your confident guesses quickly and skip the ones you are unsure about. If you have narrowed a drawing down to two possible words but cannot decide, it is often better to move on and guess someone else's drawing instead.

walkthrough.title: Draw Big and Simple
walkthrough.text: You are not entering an art contest. Other players are looking at your drawing from across a table, often while they are still drawing their own picture. Use the entire page. Make bold, thick lines. Simple shapes beat detailed sketches every time. A stick figure with a clear prop in hand communicates faster than a photorealistic portrait. The faster people recognize your drawing, the faster you get rid of your scoring tokens — and leftover tokens are negative points.

walkthrough.title: Study the Word Cards Before Drawing
walkthrough.text: Before you pick up your pencil, take a moment to re-read the words on your card. Identify the most confusable pairs. If your word is number 4 and words 3 and 5 are very similar, focus your drawing on the features that distinguish word 4 from 3 and 5. The words on other cards (A, B, or C) are usually less confusable, so you can worry less about those.

walkthrough.title: Manage Your Guessing Cards
walkthrough.text: Keep your seven guessing cards sorted by number at all times. During the frantic guessing phase, fumbling through unsorted cards wastes precious seconds. Also, track which numbers you have already played. If you realize you need a number that you already used on another player, you have made a mistake somewhere. You cannot take guessing cards back once played.

walkthrough.title: When to Stop Guessing
walkthrough.text: You do not have to guess every player's word. Sometimes the smartest move is to stop guessing and grab a high-value black token instead. If you have already guessed two or three drawings confidently, and the remaining drawings are confusing, just grab the best available black token and sit back. An extra 3-star black token is worth more than a risky guess that might make you the black sheep.

walkthrough.title: Use the Drawing Rules to Your Advantage
walkthrough.text: Remember what you CANNOT do in your drawing: no letters, no numbers, no indicating your word's position on the card, no verbal hints. But everything else is fair game. Arrows, X marks, comparison drawings, size indicators — use every visual trick you can think of. You can draw what your word is NOT by crossing it out. Creative visual communication is the heart of this game.

walkthrough.title: Adjust Difficulty for Your Group
walkthrough.text: The four difficulty decks let you customize the experience. Playing with kids or first-timers? Stick with green for all four rounds. Got a group of Pictionary veterans? Jump straight to orange or red. You can even mix levels within a game — round 1 green, round 2 yellow, and so on. If any word on a card is unknown to a player, replace the card. Everyone should have a fair shot at recognizing all 21 words.

summary.title: Drawing: Differentiate, Don't Decorate
bullets: Your goal: distinguish your word from the OTHER words on the same card
Draw the unique features ONLY your word has
Don't waste time on beauty — focus on clarity

summary.title: Speed vs. Accuracy in Guessing
bullets: First correct guesser gets highest-value token — speed matters
But wrong guesses accumulate toward black sheep penalty
Skip uncertain guesses rather than risk penalties

summary.title: Draw Big and Simple
bullets: Use the ENTIRE page with bold, thick lines
Simple shapes beat detailed sketches
Faster recognition = faster token giveaway = fewer negatives

summary.title: Study the Word Cards Before Drawing
bullets: Re-read your card's words before you draw
Identify the most confusable pairs near your word
Focus on distinguishing features

summary.title: Manage Your Guessing Cards
bullets: Keep guessing cards sorted 1-7 at all times
Track which numbers you've already played
No take-backs once a guessing card is placed

summary.title: When to Stop Guessing
bullets: You don't HAVE to guess every drawing
Grab a high-star black token instead of making a risky guess
3 confident guesses + big black token > 5 guesses with 2 wrong

summary.title: Use the Drawing Rules to Your Advantage
bullets: No letters, numbers, position hints, or verbal clues allowed
Arrows, X marks, comparisons, and size indicators are all fair game
Draw what your word is NOT and cross it out

summary.title: Adjust Difficulty for Your Group
bullets: Green (easiest) through Red (hardest) — mix and match freely
Replace any card with an unknown word
All-green is perfectly fine for casual groups

walkthrough.title: The Speed-Draw Strategy
walkthrough.text: The fastest drawer has a massive advantage in Pictomania. If you finish drawing 30 seconds before everyone else, you get first dibs on guessing — and first guesses earn the most valuable tokens. To draw fast, practice reducing your word to 2-3 essential strokes. Do not draw a cow — draw an oval with horns and an udder. Three seconds, done, and you are already scanning the table. Speed drawing also lets you grab the best black scoring token before anyone else.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Guessing Order
walkthrough.text: When you start guessing, do not just go clockwise. Start with the easiest-to-identify drawing. A quick confident guess earns you a token and frees up mental space. Then move to the next clearest drawing. Save the confusing ones for last — or skip them entirely. By going easy-to-hard, you maximize your correct guesses and minimize time wasted on drawings you might get wrong.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Room While Drawing
walkthrough.text: Here is a sneaky trick: while you are drawing, glance at what others are drawing. You do not need to finish your own drawing before you start forming guesses. Start narrowing down possibilities in your head as you draw. By the time you put your pencil down, you might already know two or three guesses. This dual-tracking of drawing and guessing separates great players from good ones.

walkthrough.title: Elimination Guessing
walkthrough.text: When you cannot tell exactly what someone is drawing, use elimination. Each player has been assigned a different number — so if you have already guessed #3 on someone else's drawing, you know this player is NOT drawing word #3. Cross-reference the word cards with the drawing and eliminate impossible words. Sometimes you can narrow it down to the correct answer purely through elimination, even if the drawing itself is terrible.

walkthrough.title: The Black Token Race
walkthrough.text: The black scoring tokens are a hidden point engine that many players undervalue. In a 5-player game, the difference between the best and worst black token can be 4 stars. That is a huge swing. If you are confident about 2-3 guesses but unsure about the rest, grab the best black token NOW rather than wasting time on risky guesses. A 5-star black token is worth more than one correct guess on a drawing where you would get a 2-star colored token.

walkthrough.title: Make Your Drawing Un-Confusable
walkthrough.text: Advanced players do not just draw their word — they actively draw AGAINST the other words on their card. If your word is 'castle' and 'house' is on the same card, draw a moat, a drawbridge, and a flag. If 'church' is also on the card, make sure it does NOT have a steeple. The goal is not just 'looks like a castle' but 'could not possibly be a house, church, or anything else on this card.' This strategic differentiation is the key to making sure every player guesses your word correctly.

walkthrough.title: Managing Your Token Economy
walkthrough.text: Tokens you give away and tokens you keep tell a story. Your colored scoring tokens start on your LEFT (negative). Every player who guesses your word correctly gets one, removing it from your negative pile. So your ideal outcome is: everyone guesses your word (zero negative tokens) AND you guess everyone else's word correctly (maximum positive tokens). Focus equally on drawing well (to clear your negatives) and guessing well (to earn positives). Neglecting either side costs you.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty Level Mastery
walkthrough.text: As difficulty increases, the words on each card become more similar. Green might have 'dog, cat, horse' — easy to differentiate. Red might have 'poodle, husky, labrador.' The key to harder difficulties is finding the ONE visual element that is unique. For 'poodle,' it is the curly pom-pom grooming. For 'husky,' it is the thick fur and sled. Practice finding that single distinguishing feature quickly, and you will dominate the harder decks while others struggle.

summary.title: The Speed-Draw Strategy
bullets: Faster drawing = first guesses = highest-value tokens
Reduce your word to 2-3 essential strokes
Speed also lets you grab the best black token first

summary.title: Strategic Guessing Order
bullets: Guess easiest drawings first, hardest last
Quick confident guesses free up mental space
Skip confusing drawings rather than risk wrong guesses

summary.title: Reading the Room While Drawing
bullets: Glance at others' drawings while you draw your own
Start forming guesses before you put your pencil down
Dual-tracking drawing + guessing = huge time advantage

summary.title: Elimination Guessing
bullets: Each player has a unique number — track which you've already guessed
Cross-reference word cards to eliminate impossible words
Can identify correct answer through elimination even if drawing is poor

summary.title: The Black Token Race
bullets: Difference between best and worst black token can be 4+ stars
Grab the best black token early if you've made 2-3 confident guesses
A 5-star black token > one correct guess earning a 2-star colored token

summary.title: Make Your Drawing Un-Confusable
bullets: Draw AGAINST the other words on your card, not just FOR yours
Add unique elements that only your word has
Goal: could not possibly be mistaken for any other word on the card

summary.title: Managing Your Token Economy
bullets: LEFT tokens (negative) clear when players guess your word correctly
RIGHT tokens (positive) earned from your correct guesses
Focus equally on drawing well (clear negatives) and guessing well (earn positives)

summary.title: Difficulty Level Mastery
bullets: Harder decks = more similar words on each card
Find the ONE visual element unique to your word
Practice quick differentiation to dominate harder difficulties

entries.definition: A double-sided card containing 7 numbered words (1-7). Three word cards are placed in the card holder each round, creating 21 possible words. Words on the same card tend to be related to each other.
A stand that holds the three word cards so all players can see them. Should be placed centrally where everyone can comfortably read all the words.
One of 6 cards labeled A through F (though typically only A, B, C are relevant for the 3 cards in the holder). Secretly tells a player which of the three word cards contains their assigned word.
One of 7 cards numbered 1 through 7. Secretly tells a player which of the seven words on their assigned word card they must draw.
A numbered card (1-7) in a player's color. Placed face down on another player's card stack to guess which word that player is drawing. Each player has all 7 guessing cards regardless of player count.
Tokens in each player's color with varying star values. Given to players who correctly guess your word. Unawarded tokens remaining on your left side count as negative points.
Bonus tokens with star values placed in the center of the table. Taken by players when they finish guessing. Normally counts as positive points, but moves to negative if you become the black sheep.
A sheet from the pad that is folded in half to create a 4-page booklet. Each page has a star where you write your round score. The gray line should be at the bottom to indicate orientation.
Colored pencils (one per player) used for drawing. A sharpener is included in the box. Pencils match each player's color.

The easiest difficulty level of word cards. Words are common and easily distinguishable. Recommended for first-time players, children, and casual groups. Used in round 1 of a standard progressive game.
The second difficulty level. Words are slightly more similar within each card, requiring more precise drawings. Used in round 2 of a standard progressive game.
The third difficulty level. Words on each card are closely related, making differentiation challenging. Used in round 3 of a standard progressive game.
The highest difficulty level. Words on each card are very similar, requiring extremely precise and creative drawings. Used in round 4 of a standard progressive game.
The core mechanic of Pictomania. All players draw at the same time, and guessing can begin as soon as any player finishes drawing. There are no turns during the drawing and guessing phase.

The pile of cards beside each player's drawing. Starts with the player's face-down letter and number cards. Other players add their guessing cards face down on top when making guesses. Order matters — earlier guesses are on the bottom (under later guesses) and earn higher-value tokens.

The player with the most wrong guesses in a round. The black sheep's black scoring token moves to the negative (left) side. If multiple players are tied for most wrong guesses, there is no black sheep that round.
Tokens earned from correct guesses on other players' drawings, plus your black scoring token (if not black sheep). Placed on the right side of your drawing. Stars are added to your score.

Your own colored scoring tokens that were NOT given away because players failed to guess your word (wrong guess or no attempt). Also includes the black sheep penalty token. Stars are subtracted from your score.
Calculated by subtracting left-side (negative) stars from right-side (positive) stars. Written in the star on your drawing paper page. Can be negative in tough rounds.
The sum of all four round scores. Written in the fifth star on the drawing paper. Highest total wins. There is no tiebreaker rule.

Restrictions on what you can draw. You cannot use letters, numerals, Morse code, or other writing systems. You cannot indicate the position of your word on the card. You cannot comment on your word or give verbal hints. Your drawing must represent the meaning of the word, not its form.
Once you have made your first guess on any player's drawing, you cannot modify your own drawing. This prevents players from adjusting their picture based on what they learn from others' guesses.
You may only place one guessing card on each player's card stack. You cannot change or take back a guess once placed. You do not guess on your own drawing.

If you accidentally draw the wrong word, you should admit it. Return all guessing cards to their players. No one gets your tokens, and those guesses are ignored for black sheep purposes. You keep and subtract your own unawarded tokens. Your guesses on others can still earn you points.

If a player guesses a number that matches a word on a completely different card than intended, and that number happens to match the drawer's actual assigned number, it still counts as correct. Both players got lucky!

An optional variant using one fewer black scoring token than the number of players. The round ends as soon as the last black token is taken, meaning one player gets no black token and cannot finish guessing. Best with 5-6 players.

You can play all four rounds at the same difficulty level or mix difficulties to suit your group. When playing with children, all-green rounds work well. Keep a discard pile to prevent card repetition across rounds.
3 players: each player uses 2 colored scoring tokens and 3 black scoring tokens are placed in the center. In the more competitive variant, use 2 black tokens.
4 players: each player uses 3 colored scoring tokens and 4 black scoring tokens are placed in the center. In the more competitive variant, use 3 black tokens.
5 players: each player uses 4 colored scoring tokens and 5 black scoring tokens are placed in the center. In the more competitive variant, use 4 black tokens.
6 players: each player uses 5 colored scoring tokens and 6 black scoring tokens are placed in the center. In the more competitive variant, use 5 black tokens.
